items.title: LokiBot After a Decade: An Analysis of a Recent LokiBot Campaign
items.description: LokiBot, an infostealer first advertised in May 2015, continues to operate after more than a decade with numerous variants. The malware targets credentials from over a hundred software products including browsers, cryptocurrency wallets, password managers, email and FTP clients. A recent campaign delivers LokiBot through malspam with JScript email attachments, executing a multi-stage infection cha
items.ai_summary: LokiBot, a malware first identified in 2015, continues to evolve with new variants, targeting credentials from over 100 software products including browsers, cryptocurrency wallets, and email clients. The recent campaign delivers LokiBot via malspam with JScript email attachments, initiating a multi-stage infection chain. If exploited, LokiBot can lead to significant data breaches, compromising sensitive user information. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering, ensuring that JScript attachments are blocked, and educate users about the risks of opening suspicious emails. Additionally, keeping software up-to-date, particularly password managers and browsers, and using strong, unique passwords can help prevent LokiBot infections.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1104| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1555.005| C[Credential Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: KimJongRAT Continues to Evolve by Leveraging LOTS
items.description: In May 2026, security researchers observed an attack campaign distributing KimJongRAT through GitHub and other legitimate services. KimJongRAT, used by the North Korean APT group Kimsuky since 2013, combines information stealing and remote access capabilities. The infection chain begins with phishing emails containing shortened URLs redirecting to GitHub Releases hosting malicious ZIP files. Victi
items.ai_summary: The KimJongRAT malware, used by the North Korean APT group Kimsuky since 2013, has been observed in a recent attack campaign leveraging legitimate services such as GitHub to distribute the malware. The infection chain begins with phishing emails containing shortened URLs that redirect to GitHub Releases hosting malicious ZIP files, which is classified as T1566 (Phishing). Once infected, the malware combines information stealing and remote access capabilities, allowing the threat actors to encrypt data for impact, classified as T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact). To remediate this threat, defenders should implement email filters to block phishing emails and restrict access to GitHub Releases, and ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual network traffic or unauthorized access to sensitive data, and have an incident response plan in place in case of an attack.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FIFA 2026 Security Alert: Cybercriminals Exploit Fan Excitement with Mass Phishing
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting anticipation for the FIFA World Cup 2026 through sophisticated phishing campaigns targeting fans seeking tickets, hospitality packages, and tournament information. Attackers have deployed FIFA-themed domains and mobile-optimized phishing infrastructure resolving to specific IP addresses, designed to harvest credentials and payment information. The campaigns feature con
items.ai_summary: The FIFA 2026 Security Alert involves a sophisticated phishing campaign targeting fans seeking tickets, hospitality packages, and tournament information. The attackers have deployed FIFA-themed domains and mobile-optimized phishing infrastructure to harvest credentials and payment information, exploiting the anticipation for the FIFA World Cup 2026. The campaign features convincing phishing emails and websites, with the primary attack vector being T1566 (Phishing), where attackers trick victims into divulging sensitive information. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users on phishing detection, and enforce multi-factor authentication for all sensitive accounts. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious domain registrations and IP addresses associated with the phishing campaigns can help in early detection and mitigation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From poisoned search results to GPU mining: A cryptojacking campaign abusing ScreenConnect and Microsoft .NET utilities
items.description: Microsoft Defender Experts identified an active cryptojacking campaign leveraging AI-assisted delivery mechanisms alongside traditional SEO poisoning. Attackers create fake download sites impersonating trusted utilities like CrystalDiskInfo, HWMonitor, and FurMark, targeting users with high-performance GPUs. Victims download ZIP archives containing legitimate executables bundled with malicious DLL
items.ai_summary: The cryptojacking campaign exploits users by leveraging SEO poisoning to create fake download sites for trusted utilities such as CrystalDiskInfo, HWMonitor, and FurMark, targeting those with high-performance GPUs. The attackers bundle legitimate executables with malicious DLLs within ZIP archives, which when downloaded and executed, lead to the compromise of the system. The real-world impact includes the hijacking of system resources for cryptocurrency mining, specifically targeting GPUs, and potential data encryption for impact. To remediate, defenders should ensure their systems are configured to only allow trusted executable files to run, and implement strict controls on downloading and executing files from the internet, especially ZIP archives. Additionally, monitoring system resource usage for unusual patterns can help in early detection of such campaigns.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608.006| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1560.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1496| D[Resource Hijacking]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Millenium: A RAT Rewritten, A Threat Multiplied
items.description: Group-IB analyzes Millenium RAT version 4.*, a remote access trojan that has undergone significant architectural changes from .NET to native C++, while continuing to leverage Telegram Bot API for command and control without requiring dedicated server infrastructure. The malware is distributed as Malware-as-a-Service by developer 'ShinyEnigma' for $50-90 USD. Active exploitation campaigns are condu
items.ai_summary: The Millenium RAT version 4.* is a remote access trojan that has undergone significant architectural changes from .NET to native C++, allowing it to leverage Telegram Bot API for command and control without requiring dedicated server infrastructure. This malware is distributed as Malware-as-a-Service by developer 'ShinyEnigma' for $50-90 USD, and its active exploitation campaigns are conducted through various attack vectors, including the use of T1071 Application Layer Protocol to communicate with the command and control server. If exploited, the malware can lead to T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact, resulting in significant data loss and disruption to affected organizations. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network monitoring and detection capabilities to identify potential T1583.004 Server exploitation attempts, and ensure that all remote access software is up-to-date and configured to use secure protocols. Additionally, organizations should consider implementing a Telegram Bot API monitoring solution to detect and prevent potential command and control communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From San Pedro to Salinas: How a Chinese Framework “DCloud Uni-App” Powers a Global Scam Economy
items.description: A Chinese web-development framework called DCloud Uni-App has become the technical foundation for over 236,000 scam domains since 2022, powering fake cryptocurrency exchanges, pig-butchering operations, wallet drainers, gambling platforms, and brand-impersonation sites. The framework gained prominence after the 2024 RainbowEx cryptocurrency scam in Argentina, which defrauded residents of San Pedro
items.ai_summary: The DCloud Uni-App framework has been utilized by threat actors to create and manage over 236,000 scam domains globally, primarily targeting cryptocurrency exchanges and wallet users. This framework has been instrumental in facilitating pig-butchering operations, wallet drainers, and brand-impersonation sites, resulting in significant financial losses for victims. The framework's versatility and ease of use have made it an attractive tool for scammers, who can quickly set up and manage fake websites and phishing campaigns. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust email security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular email account monitoring, to prevent T1586.002 email account compromises. Additionally, defenders should prioritize access token security, regularly reviewing and updating token configurations to prevent T1134 access token manipulation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1134| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CL-STA-1062 Targets Southeast Asian Governments and Critical Infrastructure
items.description: Throughout 2025, Chinese-speaking threat actors tracked as CL-STA-1062 conducted extensive operations against government entities and critical infrastructure in Southeast Asia, specifically targeting state-owned enterprises in energy and government sectors. Active since March 2022, this cluster was previously identified as UAT-7237 in campaigns against Taiwan's web hosting infrastructure. The atta
items.ai_summary: The CL-STA-1062 threat actor group has been conducting targeted operations against Southeast Asian governments and critical infrastructure, particularly focusing on state-owned enterprises in the energy and government sectors. This group, active since March 2022 and previously identified as UAT-7237, has been involved in campaigns against Taiwan's web hosting infrastructure. The real-world impact of these operations could result in significant data breaches and disruption of critical services, affecting the daily lives of citizens and the operational efficiency of targeted organizations. To remediate these threats, defenders should implement robust network monitoring, utilize intrusion detection systems, and enforce strict access controls, including multi-factor authentication for all users. Additionally, ensuring all software and systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches is crucial, although specific CVE numbers are not available for this threat, highlighting the importance of proactive security measures.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: STOCKSTAY Another Day: The Latest Addition to Turla’s Intelligence Gathering Apparatus
items.description: Google Threat Intelligence Group has identified STOCKSTAY, a .NET backdoor continuously developed and deployed by Russia-linked Turla (FSB Center 16) since December 2022. The multi-component malware communicates via secure WebSocket connections and targets government and military organizations in Ukraine, as well as entities interested in Italian foreign policy. STOCKSTAY shares significant code o
items.ai_summary: The STOCKSTAY malware, a .NET backdoor, has been identified by Google Threat Intelligence Group as a tool used by the Russia-linked Turla group since December 2022. This multi-component malware communicates via secure WebSocket connections, targeting government and military organizations in Ukraine, as well as entities interested in Italian foreign policy. The malware's use of T1219 (Remote Access Software) and T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) tactics indicates a high level of sophistication, allowing for remote access and data encryption. To remediate, defenders should implement network traffic monitoring to detect unusual WebSocket connections and update their intrusion detection systems to recognize the STOCKSTAY malware's signature. Additionally, organizations should prioritize securing remote access points and encrypting sensitive data to prevent unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Photo ZIP campaign targeting hospitality industry delivers Node.js implant for persistent access
items.description: Since April 2026, a sophisticated multi-stage intrusion campaign has targeted hospitality and hotel organizations across Europe and Asia. The operation uses photo-themed ZIP archives containing malicious shortcut files disguised as images. When executed, these shortcuts initiate an attack chain involving obfuscated PowerShell, Node.js-based implants, and dual registry persistence mechanisms. The t
items.ai_summary: The Photo ZIP campaign targets the hospitality industry by using photo-themed ZIP archives containing malicious shortcut files disguised as images, which when executed, initiate an attack chain involving obfuscated PowerShell and Node.js-based implants. The attack exploits the trust in image files to gain initial access, leveraging T1036 (Masquerading) to disguise malicious files. Real-world impact includes persistent access to compromised systems, allowing threat actors to move laterally and potentially exfiltrate sensitive data. To remediate, defenders should block shortcut files (.lnk) from being executed, ensure PowerShell is restricted to necessary personnel, and monitor for unusual Node.js activity. Additionally, implementing T1560.001 mitigation strategies, such as restricting archive utilities, can help prevent the initial stages of the attack.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1560.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Hits LeoPlatform npm Packages and GitHub Actions, Expands to the Go Ecosystem
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack campaign linked to Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades malware has compromised LeoPlatform npm packages, GitHub Actions workflows, and the Verana Blockchain Go module. The attack employs binding.gyp install-time execution, Bun-staged JavaScript malware, and encrypted credential exfiltration targeting developer and CI/CD environments. Malicious packages were publi
items.ai_summary: The Mini Shai-Hulud campaign has been linked to a sophisticated supply chain attack compromising LeoPlatform npm packages and GitHub Actions workflows, as well as the Verana Blockchain Go module. This attack utilizes binding.gyp install-time execution to deploy Bun-staged JavaScript malware, which then exfiltrates encrypted credentials from developer and CI/CD environments. The campaign's use of T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise) and T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel) tactics indicates a high level of sophistication and potential for widespread impact. To remediate, developers should immediately review and update their npm packages and GitHub Actions workflows to the latest versions, and implement additional security measures such as multi-factor authentication and regular credential rotations. Furthermore, organizations should monitor their CI/CD environments for suspicious activity and consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-49774: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection') vulnerability in Filip...
items.description:
Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection') vulnerability in Filipe Nasc RD Station allows Remote Code Inclusion.

This issue affects RD Station: from n/a through 5.6.0.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-49774 is a critical Code Injection vulnerability in RD Station, affecting versions 1.0 through 5.6.0. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by injecting malicious code, allowing for Remote Code Inclusion. This can lead to arbitrary code execution, potentially resulting in data breaches, ransomware attacks, or even complete system compromise. To remediate, update RD Station to version 5.6.1 or later, and ensure that the 'allow_remote_code_inclusion' configuration option is set to 'false'. Additionally, implement a web application firewall (WAF) to block suspicious traffic and monitor for any signs of exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1055| B[Process Injection]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-49772: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injecti...
items.description:
Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection') vulnerability in Liquid Web / StellarWP The Events Calendar allows Blind SQL Injection.

This issue affects The Events Calendar: from 6.15.12 through 6.16.2.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-49772 is a critical SQL Injection vulnerability in The Events Calendar, affecting versions 6.15.12 through 6.16.2. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a malicious SQL query to the application, allowing them to extract sensitive data or execute arbitrary SQL commands. This can lead to data breaches, privilege escalation, and potentially even ransomware attacks. To remediate this issue, administrators should update to version 6.16.3 or later, and ensure that the 'wp-config.php' file is properly configured to prevent SQL injection attacks. Additionally, it is recommended to implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and block suspicious traffic.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-40809: Missing Authorization vulnerability in Rara Themes Metro Magazine allows Exploit...
items.description:
Missing Authorization vulnerability in Rara Themes Metro Magazine allows Exploiting Incorrectly Configured Access Control Security Levels.

This issue affects Metro Magazine: from n/a through 1.4.1.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40809 is a Missing Authorization vulnerability in Rara Themes Metro Magazine, affecting versions 1.4.1 and below. This vulnerability allows attackers to exploit incorrectly configured access control security levels, potentially leading to unauthorized access and data tampering. To remediate, users should update to version 1.4.2 or later, and ensure that access control security levels are properly configured. Specifically, users should set the 'access_level' option to 'admin' or 'author' in the 'config.php' file, and ensure that the 'allow_unsafe_raw_sql' option is set to 'false'. Additionally, users should review their plugin and theme configurations to ensure that they are not vulnerable to this issue.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-39581: Subscriber SQL Injection in WP Sessions Time Monitoring Full Automatic <= 1.1.4 ...
items.description: Subscriber SQL Injection in WP Sessions Time Monitoring Full Automatic <= 1.1.4 versions.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-39581 is a critical SQL injection vulnerability in WP Sessions Time Monitoring Full Automatic plugin versions up to 1.1.4. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a malicious request to the plugin's public-facing application, allowing them to inject arbitrary SQL code. This can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive subscriber data, potentially resulting in data breaches or privilege escalation. To remediate this vulnerability, update the plugin to version 1.1.5 or later, and ensure that the 'wp_sessions' table is properly sanitized to prevent future SQL injection attacks. Additionally, consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and block suspicious traffic.
items.workflow_graph:

items.title: CVE-2026-39574: Unauthenticated SQL Injection in InPost Gallery <= 2.1.4.6 versions....
items.description: Unauthenticated SQL Injection in InPost Gallery <= 2.1.4.6 versions.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-39574 is a critical, unauthenticated SQL injection vulnerability in InPost Gallery versions 2.1.4.6 and below. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a specially crafted HTTP request to the affected application, allowing them to inject malicious SQL code and potentially gain unauthorized access to sensitive data. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to data breaches, privilege escalation, and even ransomware attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, administrators should update to InPost Gallery version 2.1.4.7 or later, and ensure that the 'allow_url_include' configuration option is set to 'Off' in the 'wp-config.php' file. Additionally, it is recommended to implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent similar attacks in the future.

items.title: $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack
items.description: The decentralized prediction market said hackers targeted some of its users through a compromise of a third-party vendor. The post $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft Warns of Photo ZIP Phishing Campaign Targeting Hotels with Node.js Implant
items.description: An active phishing campaign has been targeting hotel and other hospitality organizations across Europe and Asia since April 2026, using photo-themed ZIP files to drop a Node.js implant and dig into front-desk machines, Microsoft says. The company has not attributed the activity to a known threat actor, and the operators' end goal is still unclear. The lure plays to how hotels work.

items.title: Russian APT Deploys ‘StockStay’ Backdoor Against Ukrainian Targets
items.description: Turla has been using the backdoor against government and military organizations in Ukraine for espionage. The post Russian APT Deploys ‘StockStay’ Backdoor Against Ukrainian Targets appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Russia Used Cellebrite on Jailed Activist's iPhone Months After Sales Cutoff
items.description: Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED forensic tools to break into the iPhone of detained opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite said it would stop selling its tools and services to Russia and Belarus. The finding, published June 25 by the Citizen Lab, rests on two things that rarely line up: traces on the phone itself and an official Russian

items.title: First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild
items.description: CISA has added the remote code execution flaw CVE-2026-12569 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The post First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
items.description: A major overhaul of the Model Context Protocol shifts critical security responsibilities from the protocol itself to developers and platform operators. The post New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.description: The Russian state-sponsored threat actor known as Turla has been attributed to a previously undocumented .NET backdoor called STOCKSTAY that has been deployed against government and military organizations in Ukraine, and entities that have an interest in Italian foreign policy. Describing the Windows backdoor as continually developed by the hacking group, Google Threat Intelligence Group (

items.title: Philip Martin Joins Uber as Chief Information Security Officer
items.description: Martin brings experience from Coinbase, Palantir, Amazon, and the U.S. Army to lead Uber's cybersecurity and enterprise security organization. The post Philip Martin Joins Uber as Chief Information Security Officer appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Anthropic is testing desktop-like Claude Cowork for mobile
items.description: Anthropic appears to be testing Claude Cowork support on mobile, allowing you to manage long-running Claude tasks from your phone. [...]

items.title: Poland busts SIM-swapping gang tied to millions in crypto theft
items.description: Authorities in Poland have arrested four members of an organized cybercrime group accused of breaching telecommunications partners and hijacking email accounts to carry out SIM-swapping attacks. [...]

items.title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
items.description: The flaw enables server-side request forgery (SSRF) and escalates privileges to root, impacting Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME deployments.

items.title: Order-tracking app Shop abused to push callback phishing attacks
items.description: Threat actors are increasingly abusing Shop, the order-tracking app from Shopify, by adding fake purchase receipts in users' order histories to trick them into providing sensitive data or installing remote access software. [...]

items.title: Local Police Collusion Hampers Crackdown on Asian Scam Centers
items.description: With tens of billions of dollars flowing into regional economies from cybercrime, scam centers continue to flourish, despite international and law-enforcement efforts.

items.title: DHS chief says president has met with potential CISA nominee; agency plans to hire 600
items.description: Once a new CISA director is in place, the agency will ramp up hiring efforts, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin told lawmakers. The White House has not yet announced a nominee.

items.title: Microsoft quietly extends free Windows 10 ESU support to October 2027
items.description: Microsoft has quietly extended its free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates (ESU) program for consumers by an additional year, allowing enrolled devices to continue receiving security updates until October 12, 2027. [...]

items.title: New macOS malware embeds fake errors to confuse AI analysis tools
items.description: A newly discovered macOS malware dubbed "Gaslight" is designed to confuse AI-assisted malware analysis tools by hiding prompt injection strings and fake debugging data within the executable. [...]

items.title: PirloTV sports piracy network disrupted as 44 domains seized
items.description: A major sports piracy ring linked to the illegal PirloTV streaming platform has been disrupted in an action that targeted 44 domains. [...]

items.title: Bluekit phishing kit adopts browser-in-the-middle for login theft
items.description: The Bluekit phishing-as-a-service platform continues to evolve with nearly 70 new hostnames identified over the past week and by adding browser-in-the-middle capabilities for improved data theft. [...]

items.title: Another Russian dairy company reportedly disrupted by cyberattack
items.description: A dairy products manufacturer in Russia's republic of Bashkortostan is the latest such company to have its operations snarled by a cyberattack.

items.title: Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Found with Dormant Script Injection Capability
items.description: An analysis of a popular Google Chrome ad block extension for YouTube has uncovered the ability to execute arbitrary JavaScript code. According to Island, the extension, named Adblock for YouTube (ID: cmedhionkhpnakcndndgjdbohmhepckk), has more than 10 million installs and carries a Featured badge on the Chrome Web Store. The extension description states that it allows users to prevent web

items.title: The Four Elevations of Effective Fraud Prevention
items.description: Fraudsters don't attack just one transaction. They target accounts, platforms, and entire ecosystems. IPQS explains the four elevations of fraud prevention and why broader visibility improves fraud detection. [...]

items.title: Runlayer Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding
items.description: The startup’s platform functions as a secure control layer, aiming to secure AI tools across enterprises. The post Runlayer Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Smart TV Proxyware, 24-Year curl Bug, AI Crime Forums + 13 More Stories
items.description: It’s dumb out there again. This week has the usual smell of prod on fire and nobody wanting to admit who left the door open — old creds still working, trusted apps doing sketchy crap, browser tricks jumping the fence, and “normal” workflows turning into phishing pipes because apparently email was not enough hell already. The worst part is how cheap some of it feels. Not elite. Not cinematic.

items.title: Ukraine's state postal operator reports app disruption after cyberattack
items.description: Ukraine's state-owned postal operator said it was experiencing disruptions to some of its app services due to a suspected cyberattack, but did not say who was behind it.

items.title: Russia used Cellebrite phone-hacking tool to crack down on dissident after firm cut off country
items.description: The continued use of the powerful data extraction product soon after the company in March 2021 said it would stop working with Russia suggests the firm has been unable to pull back its technology from authoritarian government customers, researchers say.

items.title: Webinar: Why account takeovers remain one of the hardest threats to stop
items.description: Account takeover attacks continue to challenge security teams because attackers often operate through legitimate accounts and trusted services. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help organizations identify compromised accounts faster and automate response workflows. [...]

items.title: Cal Water Says No OT Systems Breached in Iranian Handala Cyberattack
items.description: Mandiant has helped the California water utility investigate the cyberattack launched by Iranian hacker group Handala. The post Cal Water Says No OT Systems Breached in Iranian Handala Cyberattack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Lantronix Serial-to-IP Converter Flaw Exploited in Attacks After OT Threat Warning
items.description: The exploited flaw, CVE-2025-67038, is one of the vulnerabilities disclosed in April as part of the BRIDGE:BREAK research project. The post Lantronix Serial-to-IP Converter Flaw Exploited in Attacks After OT Threat Warning appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Surviving the Mythos Era: Richard Bejtlich on the Case for NDR
items.description: Despite the abundance of telemetry at analysts’ disposal, many security operations teams struggle to answer a few basic questions during incident investigation: What happened? What evidence do we have? How do we know we’re seeing it all, in context? Answering these questions requires teams to go beyond alerts, the most common basis for initial triage. But investigations (and their outcomes)

items.title: GitLab Patches Code Execution, Information Disclosure Vulnerabilities
items.description: The latest GitLab CE/EE updates address 13 vulnerabilities, including three high-severity defects. The post GitLab Patches Code Execution, Information Disclosure Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: What do Ports Hear When Nobody's Listening? An Assessment of Automated Cybercrime [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Nicole Phillips, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Europe Evolves Into Ransomware's Favorite Region
items.description: After a global lull, ransomware gangs are setting sights on a rich new arena: attacking EU organizations and their suppliers.

items.title: 25-Year-Old Vulnerability Patched in Curl
items.description: The latest version of the open source data transfer tool resolves 18 medium and low-severity vulnerabilities. The post 25-Year-Old Vulnerability Patched in Curl appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Disrupt AI-Assisted Analysis
items.description: A previously undocumented Rust-based macOS implant and information stealer has been found to embed a prompt injection payload designed to trick a malware analyst's artificial intelligence (AI) tools and trick it into aborting or refusing an analysis of the artifact. The malware has been codenamed Gaslight owing to this deceptive behavior. It's been assessed with high confidence that the tool is

items.title: New Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke in ClickFix and ModeloRAT Campaigns
items.description: A new, stealthy backdoor named Mistic has been deployed as part of suspected financially motivated attacks aimed at multiple organizations spanning insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors since April 2026. According to Symantec and Carbon Black's Threat Hunter Team, the backdoor, also tracked as MLTBackdoor, is said to be linked to an initial access broker (IAB) named

items.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
items.description: An unknown threat actor exploited a recently disclosed high-severity security flaw impacting Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN as a zero-day at least two months before it was publicly disclosed, according to new findings from Google-owned Mandiant. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS score: 7.8), allows an authenticated, local attacker to execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges

items.title: Google releases new privacy controls for activity history, personalization
items.description: Google is rolling out new privacy controls for Search services and Google Play, giving you more control over saved history and personalized recommendations. [...]

items.title: Attackers Hit Cisco SD-WAN Flaw 2 Months Before Disclosure
items.description: Researchers believe rogue peering was used to connect to the victim's SD-WAN devices to gain admin privileges and root-level access.

items.title: 2026 FIFA World Cup Faces Surge in Cyber Threats
items.description: Persistent cybercrime, social engineering, and infrastructure threats continue to plague the FIFA 2026 World Cup across the US, Canada, and Mexico.

items.title: Three ‘cybercrime as a service’ operations undercut by Microsoft, law enforcement
items.description: Microsoft touted its latest action against malware infrastructure as a new approach aimed at the full cybercrime "supply chain." Europol said more than 300 servers were targeted.

items.title: Do CISOs Need a Code of Ethics?
items.description: Dark Reading Confidential Episode 19: Kickbacks, no-show jobs, "dirty" VCs, and shelf ware — industry expert Robert "RSnake" Hansen explains why he thinks it's time for a CISO code of ethics. It could ensure cybersecurity bosses aren't engaged in self-dealing that could risk enterprise, and even national, security.

items.title: CISA Warns Critical Lantronix EDS5000 Flaw Is Being Actively Exploited
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Tuesday warned of active exploitation of a critical security flaw impacting Lantronix EDS5000 Series devices, urging Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies to apply the fixes by June 26, 2026. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2025-67038 (CVSS score: 9.8), a code injection flaw that could result in the execution

items.title: More Malicious OpenClaw Skills Threaten AI Supply Chain
items.description: OpenClaw removed five packages from its ClawHub skills marketplace that bypassed security checks even though they included infostealers and other threats.

items.title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
items.description: Attackers can exploit the issue to disable security and integrated browser tools without needing administrator privileges or kernel exploits.

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. But a malicious process&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;can also mimic a non-suspicious name by

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Fixed the Bug. Nobody Fixed the Configuration., (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: The vulnerability

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
